---
title: "neffy in Canada: Same Epinephrine. New Delivery Option. | Ayel L. R. Batac"
title-block: false
canonical-url: https://ayelbatac.github.io/neffy-in-canada-same-epinephrine-new-delivery-option
description: "What Canadians with severe allergies should know about neffy, needle-free epinephrine, and emergency preparedness before anaphylaxis happens."

format:
  html:
    toc: false
    anchor-links: false
---

<style>
  h1, h2, h3, p {
    color: #1B153E;
  }
  a.study-button {
    background-color: #1B153E;
    color: white;
    padding: 0.6rem 1.2rem;
    border-radius: 6px;
    text-decoration: none;
    font-weight: bold;
    display: inline-block;
    transition: background-color 0.3s ease;
  }
  a.study-button:hover {
    background-color: #DE2221;
    color: white;
  }
</style>

# neffy in Canada: Same Epinephrine. New Delivery Option.

May 15, 2026

<div style="
  border-left: 6px solid #1B153E;
  background-color: #F4F3FA;
  padding: 1.2rem 1.5rem;
  margin: 2rem 0;
  border-radius: 4px;
">
  <strong style="color:#1B153E; font-size:1.05rem;">
    TL;DR
  </strong>
  <ul style="margin-top: 0.75rem; color:#1B153E;">
    <li><strong>neffy®</strong> is a 2 mg epinephrine nasal spray authorized in Canada for emergency treatment of allergic reactions in people who weigh 30 kg or more.</li>
    <li><strong>Epinephrine remains the first-line emergency treatment</strong> for anaphylaxis.</li>
    <li>neffy does not prevent allergic reactions; after epinephrine is used, people should <strong>call 911 or contact local emergency services</strong>.</li>
    <li>Drug-level and body-response studies support neffy as an emergency epinephrine delivery option, but real-world use during actual anaphylaxis events still needs to be studied.</li>
    <li>A new delivery option only helps if people know <strong>when to use it, how to use it, and what to do afterward</strong>.</li>
  </ul>
</div>

What changes when the medication is the same, but the way it's delivered changes?

That is the question I keep coming back to as neffy becomes available in Canada.

neffy is still epinephrine. It's still used for emergency treatment of severe allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis. The difference is how it's given: instead of being injected into the thigh, it's sprayed into one nostril.

[Health Canada has authorized neffy for emergency treatment of allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis, in adults and children who weigh 30 kg or more](https://dhpp.hpfb-dgpsa.ca/review-documents/resource/RDS1776867861872). These reactions may be triggered by insect stings or bites, foods, medications, or other allergens. They may also be idiopathic or exercise-induced. [Health Canada has not authorized neffy for patients under 30 kg](https://pdf.hres.ca/dpd_pm/00084260.PDF).

But this is not just a story about a new product. It's also about whether people are prepared to use it correctly when an emergency happens.

### What neffy is, and what it is not

neffy is a needle-free epinephrine nasal spray for emergencies. It's not an allergy cure. It doesn't prevent allergic reactions. It doesn't replace allergen avoidance, an anaphylaxis emergency plan, or the need to contact emergency services after epinephrine is used.

[According to the Canadian product monograph, the recommended dose is one spray, containing 2 mg of epinephrine, into one nostril](https://pdf.hres.ca/dpd_pm/00084260.PDF). If symptoms do not improve or symptoms worsen after the first dose, a second dose may be given in the same nostril using a new nasal spray, starting five minutes after the first dose. The product monograph also recommends that patients have immediate access to two neffy nasal sprays at all times.

A nasal spray may seem simple. Anaphylaxis is not.

In an emergency, symptoms can progress quickly. People may panic, hesitate, or second-guess what they are seeing. So even though neffy may look more familiar than an injectable device, it is still epinephrine for a potentially life-threatening situation.

For some people, neffy may be a welcome alternative to an auto-injector. For others, the best option will depend on their clinical history, preferences, access, and emergency plan.

### Why the device matters

On paper, neffy is still epinephrine. In real life, the device matters.

A person may know they are supposed to use epinephrine and still hesitate. They may be afraid of needles. A parent or caregiver may worry about using an injectable device incorrectly. Someone nearby may not be sure whether the symptoms are “bad enough”. In the middle of an emergency, even a simple set of instructions can feel harder to follow.

That is one reason neffy is important. A needle-free option may feel less intimidating for some patients, caregivers, teachers, or bystanders. It may also be easier to explain in certain community settings.

But a different device also means different training. It's not enough to ask whether someone “carries epinephrine”. We need to know which device they carry, where it's kept, who knows how to use it, and who will call emergency services afterward.

### What the evidence shows so far

The question is not whether epinephrine has a role in anaphylaxis. It does. Epinephrine is already the first-line emergency treatment.

The more specific question for neffy is whether a nasal spray can deliver epinephrine into the body in a way that supports emergency use.

[Health Canada’s product monograph states that controlled clinical trials for the treatment of anaphylaxis have not been performed with epinephrine. It also states that after one 2 mg nasal dose of neffy, epinephrine levels in the body were generally within the range seen after an epinephrine injection into the muscle over 60 minutes](https://pdf.hres.ca/dpd_pm/00084260.PDF). 

Published neffy studies provide supportive evidence about how the medication moves through the body and affects the body after use. [In a repeat-dosing study, neffy produced epinephrine levels and physiologic responses within a comparable range to intramuscular epinephrine, including in people with allergy-related nasal symptoms](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaip.2026.02.005). The same study also supported repeat dosing in the same nostril.

These studies mainly tell us what happens to epinephrine levels in the body after neffy is used. They help answer whether the medication reaches the body. They don't prove that neffy will improve real-world emergency outcomes compared with existing epinephrine devices. They also don't tell us whether every person will carry it consistently, recognize anaphylaxis quickly, or use it correctly under stress.

Broader research on intranasal adrenaline supports the scientific plausibility of nasal delivery, but not all of that evidence is specific to neffy. [A recent review concluded that clinical efficacy data remain limited, and that long-term real-world evidence on effectiveness and adherence in community settings is still needed](https://doi.org/10.1111/pai.70316).

Based on the evidence so far, neffy appears to be a reasonable emergency epinephrine option for eligible patients. But it still needs to be paired with training, clear instructions, and follow-up in real-world settings.

### What Canadians should consider next

For Canadians considering neffy, there are three practical details to sort out.

First, check eligibility. [neffy 2 mg is authorized in Canada for people who weigh 30 kg or more](https://foodallergycanada.ca/neffy-epinephrine-nasal-spray-canadian-approval-faqs/). The 1 mg dose for children under 30 kg is not available in Canada at this time.

Second, ask about cost and availability. [Food Allergy Canada has reported that neffy is expected to be available in Canadian pharmacies starting in summer 2026](https://foodallergycanada.ca/neffy-epinephrine-nasal-spray-canadian-approval-faqs/). The price will depend on coverage and reimbursement arrangements with provinces and insurance companies.

Third, update the emergency plan around the actual device. If someone carries neffy, the plan should name neffy specifically. It should say that it's a single-use nasal spray, that a second dose may be given in the same nostril with a new device after five minutes if symptoms do not improve or worsen, and that 911 or local emergency services should be called after epinephrine is used.

The device should be named in the plan, not just the medication.

Patients and families do not need to make this decision alone. An allergist, primary care provider, or pharmacist can help determine whether neffy fits their allergy history, daily life, and emergency plan.

As neffy becomes available in Canada, access is only part of the story. What matters most is whether the people closest to an allergic emergency are ready to recognize it, use the device, and act without hesitation.

-Ayel

<div style="font-size: 0.70em; margin-top: 3rem;">

**Disclaimer:** Mention of specific medications, drugs, devices, prevention strategies, or therapeutic agents in this blog is for informational and educational purposes only and does not constitute endorsement, recommendation, or promotion by the author. Clinical decisions should be made in consultation with qualified healthcare professionals and based on current evidence and applicable regulatory guidance.

The author declares no financial relationships with manufacturers of any drugs, medications, or devices mentioned in this post.

</div>

$~$

$~$
